county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Clear Creek County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Georgetown. Major silver mining center (Georgetown, Silver Plume, Idaho Springs). Georgetown Loop Railroad. One of Colorado's smallest counties by area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clear Creek County Clerk and Recorder in Georgetown holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. Georgetown and Silver Plume were major silver mining centers (Georgetown Loop Railroad era). Idaho Springs was a gold mining town. The county has exceptionally rich mining-era records including mining district records, claim filings, and assessment work documentation. The Hamill House Museum and Hotel de Paris Museum in Georgetown have mining-era collections. Georgetown Heritage Center maintains local history materials. The Colorado School of Mines in nearby Golden has mining history archives. Colorado State Archives holds Clear Creek County records on microfilm. Many ethnic communities worked the mines -- Cornish, Irish, German, Italian, and Chinese. Church records from the 1860s-1870s document mining families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Clear Creek County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Conejos County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Conejos. Oldest Catholic parish in Colorado (Our Lady of Guadalupe, 1858). Deep Hispanic heritage predating Colorado Territory. San Luis Valley. Mexican-era land grants.
county_metadata.research_tips: Conejos County Clerk and Recorder holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. This is one of the oldest settled areas in Colorado -- Our Lady of Guadalupe Parish in Conejos (established 1858) is the oldest Catholic parish in Colorado and its records are critical for Hispanic family research in the San Luis Valley. The county was part of the Mexican land grant era; the Conejos Land Grant and Sangre de Cristo Grant affect property research. Many families trace ancestry to colonial New Mexico. Spanish-language church records (baptisms, marriages, burials) predate Anglo settlement. The Fort Garland Museum (nearby) documents military and settlement history. Colorado State Archives has county records. Check New Mexico archives for pre-territorial family records. The Archdiocese of Denver holds some early parish registers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Conejos County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Costilla County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is San Luis (oldest town in Colorado, 1851). Sangre de Cristo Land Grant. Hispanic families trace to colonial New Mexico. Communal land traditions (La Vega).
county_metadata.research_tips: Costilla County Clerk and Recorder in San Luis holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. San Luis (founded 1851) is the oldest continuously inhabited town in Colorado. The county's Hispanic heritage predates Colorado Territory by decades -- many families trace to colonial New Mexican settlers. The Sangre de Cristo Land Grant (1843) is central to property research; common lands (La Vega) have a unique communal ownership history. San Luis Museum and Cultural Center has local materials. Catholic parish records at Sangre de Cristo Parish (from the 1850s) are essential. Many records are in Spanish. The Costilla County Conservancy District records document water rights, which are critical in this arid region. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. Check New Mexico territorial archives for pre-1861 family connections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Costilla County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Crowley County formed 1911 from Otero County. County seat is Ordway. Named for Senator John Crowley. Sugar beet farming. Site of the Amache (Granada) Japanese American internment camp during WWII.
county_metadata.research_tips: Crowley County Clerk and Recorder in Ordway holds records from 1911 (created from Otero County). Check Otero County for pre-1911 records. The county's agricultural economy was transformed by sugar beet farming, which brought German-Russian, Hispanic, and Japanese immigrant workers. The Amache (Granada) Japanese American internment camp (1942-1945) was located in the county -- internee records are at NARA and the Amache Preservation Society maintains a database of internees. This is a critical source for Japanese American family research in Colorado. The Crowley County Heritage Society has local materials. Colorado State Archives holds county records on microfilm. FamilySearch has indexed some Colorado records covering this area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Crowley County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Custer County formed 1877 from Fremont County. County seat is Westcliffe. Named for General George Custer. Wet Mountain Valley ranching. Silver Cliff mining boom (1870s-1880s). German colonists. Small population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Custer County Clerk and Recorder in Westcliffe holds records from 1877 (created from Fremont County). A small mountain county in the Wet Mountain Valley with ranching and limited mining heritage. Silver Cliff was briefly a booming silver mining town in the early 1880s before the silver crash. German colonists settled the Wet Mountain Valley area. Church records (Lutheran, Catholic, Methodist) document these communities. Check Fremont County for pre-1877 records. BLM GLO records document homesteads and mining claims. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. The Wet Mountain Tribune newspaper has been published since 1883. The county's small population means many families are well-documented in local histories.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Custer County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Delta County formed 1883 from Gunnison County. County seat is Delta. Former Ute homeland (removed 1881). Agriculture (fruit orchards in Paonia and Hotchkiss). Coal mining near Somerset and Paonia. Diverse immigrant mining communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Delta County Clerk and Recorder holds records from 1883 (created from Gunnison County). The county was Ute homeland until the Ute removal in 1881; early Anglo settlement followed immediately. The town of Delta was founded as an agricultural center on the Gunnison and Uncompahgre rivers. Fruit orchards (Paonia, Hotchkiss area) drew diverse settlers. Coal mining near Somerset and Paonia brought immigrant workers (Italian, Austrian, Slavic). Delta County Historical Society Museum has local collections. Paonia Public Library has North Fork Valley history materials. Check Gunnison County for pre-1883 records. Colorado State Archives holds microfilmed county records. BLM GLO records document homestead entries. Church records from various denominations serve the small communities.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Delta County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Denver County (City and County of Denver) formed 1902 from Arapahoe County as a consolidated city-county. State capital since 1867. Colorado's largest city and primary urban center. Diverse immigrant communities. Center of the 1858-1859 Pike's Peak gold rush. Hub for Rocky Mountain West genealogical research.
county_metadata.research_tips: Denver County (City and County of Denver) Clerk and Recorder holds records from 1902 when Denver separated from Arapahoe County as a consolidated city-county. For pre-1902 records, check Arapahoe County. Denver Public Library's Western History and Genealogy Department is one of the premier genealogical research centers in the Rocky Mountain West, with city directories (from 1859), photograph collections, manuscript collections, newspaper indexes, and maps. The Colorado State Archives is also located in Denver. History Colorado (formerly Colorado Historical Society) has extensive manuscript and photograph collections. Denver's diverse immigrant communities -- German, Irish, Italian, Jewish, Chinese, Japanese, Hispanic, African American -- are documented through ethnic newspapers, church records, organizational records, and neighborhood histories. The Blair-Caldwell African American Research Library documents Denver's Black community. Fairmount Cemetery and Riverside Cemetery records are valuable. Denver District Court probate records are at the state archives.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Denver County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Dolores County formed 1881 from Ouray County. County seat is Dove Creek. Named for the Dolores River. Small and remote southwestern county. Rico silver mining. Ancestral Puebloan archaeological sites. One of Colorado's least populated counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dolores County Clerk and Recorder in Dove Creek holds records from 1881 (created from Ouray County). A small, remote county in southwestern Colorado with ranching and limited mining heritage. Rico was a silver mining town in the 1870s-1880s (now in the county though it was originally part of Ouray County). The county has one of the smallest populations in Colorado. Ancestral Puebloan sites are present in the area. Mormon settlers from Utah established some communities. Check Ouray County for pre-1881 records. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. BLM GLO records document land patents. The Dolores County area had limited newspaper coverage -- check regional papers like the Dolores Star or Dove Creek Press for vital records and legal notices.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Dolores County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Douglas County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Castle Rock. Named for Senator Stephen Douglas. Historically ranching; rapid suburban growth since the 1990s as part of the Denver metro area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Douglas County Clerk and Recorder in Castle Rock holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. The county was historically a ranching area (notable ranch families include the Schweiger and Cantril families). Castle Rock was a railroad stop on the Denver & Rio Grande. The Douglas County History Research Center at the Philip S. Miller Library in Castle Rock is an outstanding local genealogy resource with indexed records, photograph collections, and oral histories. Cherokee Ranch and Castle Foundation has historical ranch records. The county's rapid suburban growth since the 1990s has brought new interest in historical records. Colorado State Archives has early territorial and county records on microfilm. BLM GLO homestead records document early settlers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Douglas County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Eagle County formed 1883 from Summit County. County seat is Eagle. Mining and ranching heritage transformed by Vail ski resort (1962). Railroad history (Denver & Rio Grande). Red Cliff was original county seat.
county_metadata.research_tips: Eagle County Clerk and Recorder holds records from 1883 (created from Summit County). Originally a mining and ranching county, Eagle County became world-famous for the Vail ski resort (opened 1962). The town of Minturn was a railroad junction for the Denver & Rio Grande. Red Cliff was the original county seat and a mining camp. Gilman was a zinc/lead mining town (company town, now a ghost town). Hispanic sheepherding families worked the area from the late 1800s. Eagle County Historical Society has local collections. Check Summit County for pre-1883 records. Colorado State Archives holds microfilmed county records. The Eagle Valley Library District has regional history materials. Mining claim records and BLM GLO patents document settlers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Eagle County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.repository: Eagle County District Court / Colorado State Archives, Denver
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships, and probate proceedings for Eagle County; District Court handles probate in Colorado; older records may be at Colorado State Archives on microfilm

source_profiles.source_name: Eagle County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO Records
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Eagle County including homestead entries and mining claim patents; searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov; mining claims are especially numerous in this county

source_profiles.source_name: Eagle County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Eagle County from 1890; 1885 Colorado state census is an important supplement; check parent county for earlier census records

source_profiles.source_name: Eagle County Mining District Records and Claim Filings
source_profiles.repository: Colorado State Archives / Eagle County Clerk and Recorder / BLM
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mining district minutes, claim records, assessment work filings, and mining company records for Eagle County; mining districts kept their own records before formal county recording; Colorado State Archives and History Colorado hold many mining-era records

source_profiles.source_name: Ute Nation Records -- Eagle County Area
source_profiles.repository: NARA, Denver / Southern Ute Indian Tribe / Ute Mountain Ute Tribe / History Colorado
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Pre-removal Ute records for the Eagle County area including treaty records, agency records, annuity rolls, and correspondence; Ute removal from western Colorado completed by 1881; Southern Ute and Ute Mountain Ute reservations in southwestern Colorado retain tribal records; NARA Denver holds federal Indian agency records
